content.text: Rate Limit and Capacity Planning for LLM APIs

Every production LLM deployment collides with the three-dimensional rate limit enforced by the provider: requests per minute (RPM), tokens per minute (TPM), and requests per day (RPD). For single-tenant experimental systems these limits are easily avoided, but multi-tenant agentic platforms — where dozens of teams share a single API account — can saturate any dimension unpredictably. Systematic capacity planning prevents runaway 429 cascades and ensures your SLAs survive peak load.

attrs.alt: Rate Limit Capacity Planning: Multi-Tenant Queue Architecture
attrs.title: Token bucket queue feeding a rate limiter that enforces RPM/TPM/RPD against the LLM provider tier

content.text: Provider Rate Limit Taxonomy
The Three Limit Dimensions
Understanding which dimension bites first is the prerequisite to every capacity calculation:
RPM (Requests Per Minute)
— the number of API calls within any rolling 60-second window. RPM is the most frequently hit limit for high-concurrency systems that send many short prompts such as classifiers or routing agents.
TPM (Tokens Per Minute)

— total prompt + completion tokens across all requests in a rolling window. TPM is the binding constraint for workloads with large context windows, multi-turn conversations, or heavy retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines.
RPD (Requests Per Day)

— cumulative requests over a 24-hour window, typically a hard ceiling enforced at the account level. RPD is rarely the first constraint hit but becomes critical for batch pipelines that run long overnight jobs.
Tier Structures
Providers graduate accounts through tiers as spend or usage history accumulates. Anthropic's tier structure (approximate, subject to change) illustrates the progression:

The key insight is that tier upgrades are not simply linear — each tier roughly doubles or quadruples the ceiling, so an under-provisioned system that needs Tier 4 cannot be patched by adding a second Tier 2 account; the arithmetic does not work because both accounts share the same upstream provider ceiling.
Capacity Planning Math for Multi-Tenant Systems
Workload Profile
Model your demand from first principles rather than guessing. The relevant parameters are:
Peak concurrent users
— the number of simultaneous sessions at traffic peak (not daily active users).
Requests per user per minute
— driven by your UX: a copilot with a streaming response re-fires every 30 seconds on average; a batch pipeline fires continuously.
Tokens per request
— measure this empirically. The average is heavily right-skewed: a minority of requests with long RAG context can consume 10× the mean.
Daily active users and daily request budget
— needed to compute the RPD headroom independently of the RPM analysis.
The Safety Factor
Apply a safety factor of at least 1.25 to all calculated requirements before selecting a tier. This accounts for:
Prompt length growth as system prompts are enriched over time.
Seasonal burst days (product launches, end-of-quarter reporting).
New agent archetypes added mid-tier without a quota renegotiation cycle.
Model routing failures that cause retries, temporarily doubling effective RPM.
Python Rate Limit Calculator
Queue-Based Rate Limiting Implementation
Why You Need a Queue

Client-side retry with exponential backoff is necessary but not sufficient: it only handles individual 429 responses. Under sustained overload it causes thundering-herd storms — all retrying clients fire simultaneously after their backoff interval, immediately regenerating the overload. A queue interposes a drain rate between your tenants and the provider, smoothing the flow and preventing the retry storm.
Token Bucket Design

The token bucket algorithm is the canonical solution. Your proxy maintains two buckets: one refilled at rate RPM/60 per second, another refilled at rate TPM/60 per second. An incoming request must acquire a slot from both buckets simultaneously. If either is empty, the request waits.

import asyncio
import time
from collections import deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Awaitable, Callable


@dataclass
class RateLimitConfig:
    rpm: int
    tpm: int
    rpd: int


@dataclass(order=True)
class PrioritisedRequest:
    priority: int
    created_at: float = field(compare=False)
    tokens_estimate: int = field(compare=False)
    coro: Callable[[], Awaitable] = field(compare=False)


class TokenBucketRateLimiter:
    def __init__(self, config: RateLimitConfig):
        self.config = config
        self._minute_requests: deque[float] = deque()
        self._minute_tokens: deque[tuple[float, int]] = deque()
        self._day_requests: int = 0
        self._day_start: float = time.time()
        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()

    def _purge_old(self, now: float) -> None:
        cutoff = now - 60.0
        while self._minute_requests and self._minute_requests[0] < cutoff:
            self._minute_requests.popleft()
        while self._minute_tokens and self._minute_tokens[0][0] < cutoff:
            self._minute_tokens.popleft()
        if now - self._day_start >= 86_400:
            self._day_requests = 0
            self._day_start = now

    def _check_and_reserve(self, tokens: int) -> bool:
        now = time.time()
        self._purge_old(now)
        current_rpm = len(self._minute_requests)
        current_tpm = sum(t for _, t in self._minute_tokens)
        if (
            current_rpm >= self.config.rpm
            or current_tpm + tokens > self.config.tpm
            or self._day_requests >= self.config.rpd
        ):
            return False
        self._minute_requests.append(now)
        self._minute_tokens.append((now, tokens))
        self._day_requests += 1
        return True

    async def acquire(self, tokens: int, max_wait: float = 60.0) -> bool:
        deadline = time.time() + max_wait
        while time.time() < deadline:
            async with self._lock:
                if self._check_and_reserve(tokens):
                    return True
            await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        return False
Priority Queue Integration
Assign every incoming LLM request a priority before it enters the queue:
CRITICAL
— synchronous user-facing requests with a sub-second response SLA.
HIGH
— interactive agents where users are waiting but 2–3 second latency is acceptable.
NORMAL
— background agentic tasks that should complete within minutes.
BATCH
— overnight pipelines that have hours of tolerance.

When the queue fills, shed BATCH requests first by converting them to asynchronous jobs with a callback mechanism. Only escalate to rejecting NORMAL requests if the queue remains full for more than 30 seconds.
Overflow Strategies
Strategy Comparison
Exponential backoff with jitter
— the minimum viable strategy. Add a random jitter of ±25% to the backoff interval to break synchrony across clients. Start at 500 ms, cap at 30 s, retry up to 5 times.
Request coalescing
— if multiple tenants request semantically identical completions within a 100 ms window, de-duplicate them and fan out the single response. Effective for high-volume classification or routing agents.
Model fallback routing
— on a 429 from the primary model, route to a cheaper, smaller model that runs on a separate quota. Acceptable for tasks where quality degrades gracefully.
Graceful queue-depth backpressure
— when queue depth exceeds a threshold, return an HTTP 503 to new arrivals immediately (fail-fast) rather than allowing unbounded queue growth that misleads callers about their wait time.
Webhook / async pattern
— for BATCH tasks, accept the request synchronously (200 OK with a job ID), process asynchronously, and deliver results via webhook. The caller is never blocked on the queue.
YAML Capacity Plan Template

# capacity-plan.yaml — LLM API capacity plan for multi-tenant deployment
version: "1"
provider: anthropic
target_tier: "Tier 4"   # 4000 RPM / 800K TPM / 500K RPD

workload:
  peak_concurrent_users: 200
  avg_rps_per_user: 0.5          # requests per minute per concurrent user
  avg_tokens_per_request: 2000
  daily_active_users: 1500
  requests_per_user_per_day: 30

derived_requirements:            # computed by RateLimitCalculator
  required_rpm: 130              # 200 * 0.5 * 1.30
  required_tpm: 260000           # 130 * 2000
  required_rpd: 58500            # 1500 * 30 * 1.30

rate_limit_layers:
  api_gateway:
    per_tenant_rpm: 20
    per_tenant_tpm: 40000
    burst_factor: 2.0            # allow 40 rpm for up to 10 s per tenant
    overflow_action: queue

  queue:
    backend: redis
    max_depth: 5000
    priority_levels: [CRITICAL, HIGH, NORMAL, BATCH]
    drain_rate_rpm: 130
    max_wait_seconds: 120
    overflow_action: reject_lowest_priority

  retry_policy:
    initial_delay_ms: 500
    max_delay_ms: 30000
    backoff_factor: 2.0
    jitter: true
    max_retries: 5
    retryable_status_codes: [429, 503, 504]

observability:
  metrics:
    - name: llm_request_queue_depth
      alert_threshold: 1000
    - name: llm_rpm_utilisation_pct
      alert_threshold: 85
    - name: llm_tpm_utilisation_pct
      alert_threshold: 80
    - name: llm_p95_queue_wait_ms
      alert_threshold: 10000
  dashboards: [grafana]
  traces: [opentelemetry]
Observability and Tuning
Key Metrics
llm_rpm_utilisation_pct
— rolling 1-minute RPM as a percentage of tier limit. Alert at 85%.
llm_tpm_utilisation_pct
— rolling 1-minute TPM utilisation. Alert at 80% (TPM is harder to shed quickly).
llm_request_queue_depth
— number of requests waiting in queue. Alert at 1 000 or a depth that implies >30 s wait.
llm_p95_queue_wait_ms
— 95th-percentile time a request spends in queue before dispatching to the provider. Directly reflects SLA impact.
llm_429_rate
— 429 responses per minute from the provider. Non-zero sustained values indicate the queue drain rate exceeds the tier limit.
Continuous Right-Sizing

Review the bottleneck dimension monthly. If RPM headroom is consistently above 50% but TPM utilisation is above 80%, you are on the wrong tier — a tier with higher TPM but lower RPM multiplier may be available as a custom arrangement. Document the bottleneck in your capacity plan and use it as the opening argument when negotiating a limit increase with your provider.
Summary
RPM, TPM, and RPD are independent constraints; identify which dimension is your binding constraint before selecting a tier.
Calculate required capacity from a workload profile (peak concurrent users × request rate × token size) and multiply by a 1.25–1.30 safety factor.
Use a token bucket queue to smooth multi-tenant demand and prevent thundering-herd retry storms.
Assign request priorities and shed BATCH work first when the queue fills.
Monitor RPM/TPM utilisation, queue depth, and P95 wait time; alert before hitting the ceiling.
Re-evaluate the bottleneck dimension monthly and negotiate custom limits where standard tiers are poorly shaped for your workload.
